questions.question: What is structure initialization in C? Briefly explain two different methods to initialize the members of a structure variable.
keyPoints: Structure initialization is the process of assigning initial values to the members of a structure variable at the time of its declaration.
Method 1: Using an initializer list, e.g., `struct Student s1 = {101, "Alice", 87.5};`
Method 2: Using designated initializers to specify which member is being initialized, e.g., `struct Student s1 = {.name = "Bob", .rollno = 102, .marks = 92.0};`

questions.question: Compare and contrast compile-time initialization and run-time initialization of structure members. Provide a code example for each method to illustrate the difference.
keyPoints: Compile-time initialization assigns values at the point of declaration, using an initializer list. The values are hardcoded into the program.
Run-time initialization assigns values after declaration, typically using the dot operator (.) and assignment statements, often with user input or calculated values.
Code Example Run-time: `struct Date eventDate; eventDate.day = 20; eventDate.month = 12; eventDate.year = 2024;` or using `scanf("%d", &eventDate.day);`

questions.question: A programmer writes: `struct Node *head = NULL;`. Explain the significance of this initialization in the context of building a linked list. Furthermore, describe the potential consequences, including memory-related errors, if a pointer to a structure is used without being properly initialized to either NULL or a valid memory address.
keyPoints: This initialization sets the 'head' pointer to NULL, signifying an empty linked list. It is a crucial starting state before any nodes are inserted.
Using an uninitialized (dangling) pointer is undefined behavior. The pointer holds a garbage memory address.
Consequence 1: Attempting to dereference the pointer (`head->data` or `head->next`) will likely lead to a segmentation fault, crashing the program.
Consequence 2: If the program does not crash, it may corrupt data in an arbitrary memory location, leading to subtle and difficult-to-diagnose bugs.
Proper initialization to NULL allows for safety checks, e.g., `if (head == NULL)` to handle the empty list case explicitly and prevent dereferencing a garbage address.
